site.name: ANARCHISM.AFRICA
site.tagline: an afrofuturist 360° on afro-anarchism — Africa & diaspora
site.data_provenance: All works listed here are real, published cultural artifacts attributed to their creators. Community-dependent sections (events, ambassadors, services, jobs, community posts, crowdfund campaigns, merch SKUs) are empty until real submissions arrive — no fabricated entries.

hero.title: Sankofa
hero.subtitle: Haile Gerima · 1993 · Ethiopia/Ghana/USA
hero.summary: A fashion model is pulled into the past on the slave coast of Ghana — Gerima's landmark film on memory, return, and rebellion.

hero.title: How Europe Underdeveloped Africa
hero.subtitle: Walter Rodney · 1972 · essay/book chapter
hero.summary: Rodney's foundational thesis on capitalism, colonialism, and the structural impoverishment of the African continent — still naming the room.

hero.title: Zombie
hero.subtitle: Fela Kuti & Africa '70 · 1976
hero.summary: An Afrobeat broadside against the Nigerian military — and the most-banned song of its decade.

hero.title: African Anarchism: The History of a Movement
hero.subtitle: Sam Mbah & I.E. Igariwey · 1997
hero.summary: The reference text for African anarchist tradition — written from Lagos, published by See Sharp Press.

hero.title: Concerning Violence
hero.subtitle: Göran Hugo Olsson · 2014 · based on Fanon
hero.summary: Nine scenes of anti-imperial self-defence, narrated by Lauryn Hill from the pages of Frantz Fanon's The Wretched of the Earth.

hero.title: Tezeta (Nostalgia)
hero.subtitle: Mulatu Astatke · 1972
hero.summary: The defining piece of Ethio-jazz — vibraphone, tezeta scale, Addis at golden hour.

hero.title: Anarchism and the Black Revolution
hero.subtitle: Lorenzo Kom'boa Ervin · 1979
hero.summary: Written from a U.S. federal prison, Ervin's pamphlet remains the most-circulated Black-anarchist text in English.

hero.title: The Wretched of the Earth
hero.subtitle: Frantz Fanon · 1961
hero.summary: Fanon's final work — colonialism, decolonial violence, and the psychology of liberation. Preface by Jean-Paul Sartre.

films.title: Sankofa
films.summary: A fashion model on a photo-shoot at Cape Coast Castle is pulled back into slavery and resistance. Gerima's landmark film of return and rebellion.

films.title: Lumumba
films.summary: Patrice Lumumba's first 200 days as Prime Minister of independent Congo — and the assassination that ended them.

films.title: Concerning Violence
films.summary: Archive footage from Africa's liberation wars, scored to Frantz Fanon's text, narrated by Lauryn Hill — produced with Mahmood Mamdani.

films.title: The Black Power Mixtape 1967–1975
films.summary: Lost Swedish television footage of the Black Power era — Stokely Carmichael, Angela Davis, Bobby Seale — re-scored by Questlove and Om'Mas Keith.

films.title: I Am Not Your Negro
films.summary: James Baldwin's unfinished manuscript Remember This House, voiced by Samuel L. Jackson — Medgar, Malcolm, Martin.

films.title: Touki Bouki
films.summary: Two young lovers in Dakar, dreaming of Paris on a stolen motorbike — the founding text of African new-wave cinema.

films.title: Atlantique
films.summary: On the outskirts of Dakar, a girl waits for a boy who left across the sea — Diop's debut, Cannes Grand Prix 2019.

films.title: Soleil Ô
films.summary: A Mauritanian arrives in Paris and discovers the white sun of post-colonial Europe — Hondo's blistering debut, restored 2017.

films.title: Capitaine Thomas Sankara
films.summary: Documentary built from 30 hours of original Burkinabè state archive — Sankara's four-year revolution and assassination.

films.title: Faat Kiné
films.summary: A single mother running a Dakar petrol station — Sembène's portrait of African women's everyday heroism.

films.title: Yeelen (Brightness)
films.summary: A young Bambara man is hunted by his sorcerer father across pre-colonial Mali — Jury Prize, Cannes 1987.

films.title: Mandabi
films.summary: An unemployed man in Dakar receives a money order — and runs aground on a colonial bureaucracy that survived independence.

articles.title: How Europe Underdeveloped Africa
articles.author: Walter Rodney
articles.summary: The structural argument that European capitalism actively underdeveloped Africa — published in Dar es Salaam, the year before Rodney was deported.

articles.title: Anarchism and the Black Revolution
articles.author: Lorenzo Kom'boa Ervin
articles.summary: Written from federal prison and revised across decades — the most-circulated Black-anarchist text in English.

articles.title: The Master's Tools Will Never Dismantle the Master's House
articles.author: Audre Lorde
articles.summary: Lorde's keynote at the Second Sex Conference — a Black-feminist refusal of the academic-feminist ground rules.

articles.title: Discourse on Colonialism
articles.author: Aimé Césaire
articles.summary: Césaire's anti-colonial pamphlet from Martinique — the text that named the boomerang of colonial violence returning to Europe as fascism.

articles.title: Pan-African Social Ecology
articles.author: Modibo Kadalie
articles.summary: Conversations with Andrew Zonneveld — autonomy, ecology, and African mass-democratic traditions.

articles.title: On National Culture
articles.author: Frantz Fanon
articles.summary: Chapter from The Wretched of the Earth — the role of culture in liberation and the trap of mere nativism.

articles.title: A Soldier's Story: Writings by a Revolutionary New Afrikan Anarchist
articles.author: Kuwasi Balagoon
articles.summary: Black Liberation Army veteran turned anarchist — collected essays, communiqués and trial statements.

articles.title: Decolonising the Mind: The Politics of Language in African Literature
articles.author: Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o
articles.summary: Why Ngũgĩ stopped writing in English — the politics of language as the politics of mind.

articles.title: Black Marxism: The Making of the Black Radical Tradition
articles.author: Cedric J. Robinson
articles.summary: Robinson's argument for racial capitalism — and for a Black radical tradition that long predates Marxism.

articles.title: Necropolitics
articles.author: Achille Mbembe
articles.summary: Mbembe's diagnosis of sovereignty as the power to dictate who may live and who must die.

articles.title: Anarchist Panther: An Interview with Ashanti Alston
articles.author: Ashanti Alston
articles.summary: Former Black Panther and BLA prisoner on his turn to anarchism, with the Institute for Anarchist Studies.

articles.title: Griot in the Rubble: Boko Yout's Afro-Grunge as Diaspora Ritual
articles.author: ANARCHISM.AFRICA Editorial
articles.summary: Born of Togolese and Mozambican roots in the whiteness of provincial Sweden, Paul Adamah makes music that functions as riot and ritual — a genre-demolishing collision of ancestral memory, punk rebellion, vodún reclamation, and radical self-making.

embeds.why: Official video — GUSTO (title track, debut album 2025)

articles.body:
Paul Adamah was born in 1998 in Örebro, a mid-sized Swedish city that appears in statistical surveys and little else. His father came from Togo. His mother from Mozambique. In a city where Protestantism and whiteness shaped the ambient culture, Adamah learned early what diaspora children learn: how to shapeshift. How to shrink. How to translate yourself into whatever register a room demands.

It is from this quiet labour of self-erasure that Boko Yout was born.

**The Griot Tradition**

In West African culture, the griot is not merely a musician. The griot is a living archive — keeper of genealogy, political commentator, praise-singer and elegist. Their knowledge is preserved not in libraries but in the body, in the breath, in the performance. To be a griot is to be a node in a network that spans centuries, carrying what colonialism tried to silence in the one register that cannot be fully colonised: song.

Adamah named his 2024 collaborative EP _Griot_ without irony. The project, recorded with Stockholm-based producer boerd (Bård Ericson), fuses trip-hop electronics with raw vocal confessional — Ericson's lush, melodic textures holding space for Adamah's unflinching honesty. The EP arrived as both an exercise in cross-diaspora collaboration and a statement of method: this is music as ancestral conversation. The two met at a Way Out West gig, bonded over a shared reverence for British trip-hop's emotional directness, and retreated to build something intimate and strange. What emerged feels simultaneously ancient and immediate — as if the 1990s Portishead archives had been passed through a West African mouth and given new teeth.

**Afro-Grunge as Refusal**

Boko Yout's self-coined genre tag — Afro-Grunge — is not marketing. It is a refusal.

Grunge, as a form, was always about refusing polish. About breaking the guitar in ways that weren't fashionable. About using distortion not for stadium spectacle but to excavate discomfort. Boko Yout takes that same formal refusal and runs it through a different history — not the white working-class Pacific Northwest, but the Black European diaspora, the children of colonised nations navigating the metropoles of their former colonisers.

Afro-Grunge is what happens when the emotional rawness of punk meets the sonic memory of the African continent. It is what happens when a child of Togo and Mozambique picks up a distorted guitar in Sweden and refuses to make it sound like anything other than exactly what it is. The band — now a full four-piece with Joel Kiviaho, Kevin Stierne and guitarist Lionel Turner alongside Adamah — approaches guitar chaos with the mindset of a cinematographer: every sound a visual choice, every riff a narrative decision.

**GUSTO and the Therapy of Becoming**

The debut album _GUSTO_, released September 5, 2025, is framed as a series of therapy sessions. The fictional Dr. Gusto is the analyst. Adamah's alter ego Mordecai is the patient. The 13 tracks are the sessions — self-interrogation conducted at full volume, clocking in at just under 35 minutes.

This framing is not accidental. The language of therapy — interiority, excavation, the naming of what hurts — is a deeply political act for a Black artist navigating white cultural space. Frantz Fanon understood this when he wrote that the colonised subject internalises the coloniser's gaze until it becomes their own inner voice. The work of decolonisation is always also psychological: the work of distinguishing your own voice from the one that was installed in you.

_GUSTO_ performs this work noisily. The title track has been described as a collision between Queens of the Stone Age, Nine Inch Nails, and The Prodigy — but that description only captures the texture. The content is stranger and more specific: a man demanding the right to take up space, to expand, to be ungovernable in his own body and imagination. _9-2-5_ takes the grammar of the labour anthem and warps it into something more destabilising. _DEMOLITION MAN_ is exactly what it sounds like.

"I like the term joyful expansion," Adamah said in 2025. "Always acting out of your greatest desire or greatest joy. That's what I'm doing now."

**Vodún and the Reclamation of Fear**

Part of what makes Boko Yout's project distinctly post-colonial is its relationship to vodún — the spiritual tradition his Togolese father practiced. Adamah has spoken about fearing it as a child, shaped by the ambient Protestant culture of Örebro into suspicion of his own inheritance. That fear has given way to understanding and deliberate reclamation.

This is an old story, replayed in every diaspora: the child who grows into the parent's tradition after being taught to fear it. Colonialism's most effective tool was not the gun but the shame — the coding of African spiritual life as primitive, dangerous, backwards. The reclamation of vodún is therefore not merely spiritual. It is political. It is the refusal of that shame.

**The Body as Stage**

Live, Boko Yout is kinetic and generous. Adamah has described overcoming a deep fear of moving his body onstage — of taking up space physically. "I used to be scared to move a certain way," he has said. "As soon as I embraced that I was just me, this queer superstar, people started moshing and dancing naturally."

The body onstage is always political. For a Black queer artist performing in predominantly white European venues, to refuse to shrink — to mosh, to expand, to be ungovernable — is to enact the same politics that Boko Yout encodes in the music itself. It is the same politics the griot enacted, village to village, century to century: refusing to disappear.

**Griot, Again**

Boko Yout has received Newcomer of the Year nominations at the Swedish Grammis and Artist of the Future recognition at the P3 Guld Awards. These institutions are mainstream European music infrastructure — precisely the apparatus one might expect to misread what Boko Yout is doing.

And yet visibility matters. Not because institutions validate art, but because visibility creates lineage. It ensures that the next child of Togo and Mozambique growing up in Örebro or Gothenburg or Rotterdam will be able to point at something and say: this is possible. This is what it can look like. The griot's role is not only to preserve the past. It is to name what the future might become.

_GUSTO_ is, in the end, an album about exactly that: the possibility of becoming something you were told you couldn't be, in the exact register you were told to suppress.

Boko Yout is the noise that makes that possible.

music.title: Zombie
music.summary: 12 minutes of horn-line indictment of the Nigerian military — the most-banned song of its decade.

music.title: Tezeta (Nostalgia)
music.summary: The defining piece of Ethio-jazz, from Mulatu of Ethiopia — vibraphone, tezeta scale, Addis at dusk.

music.title: Pata Pata
music.summary: Makeba's international anti-apartheid anthem — recorded in New York, banned at home.

music.title: Stimela (The Coal Train)
music.summary: Nine minutes on the migrant labour trains pulling Africa's miners into apartheid mines.

music.title: Africa Unite
music.summary: From Survival — Marley's pan-African call, recorded the year before the Zimbabwe independence concert.

music.title: My Queen Is Harriet Tubman
music.summary: From Your Queen Is a Reptile — tenor sax, two drum kits, tuba, no compromise.

music.title: Monsters You Made
music.summary: Lagos Afro-fusion broadside on the violence the post-colonial state inherits from its colonisers.

music.title: Mississippi Goddam
music.summary: Written after the murder of Medgar Evers and the bombing of Birmingham — a show-tune turned indictment.

music.title: The Revolution Will Not Be Televised
music.summary: Scott-Heron's spoken-word salvo against media co-optation — congas, flute, no studio polish.

music.title: Fire on the Mountain
music.summary: From Lagos via Paris — a quiet warning about the soldiers waiting for orders.

books.title: The Wretched of the Earth
books.author: Frantz Fanon
books.summary: Fanon's last work — colonialism, decolonial violence, and the psychology of liberation. Preface by Jean-Paul Sartre.

books.title: How Europe Underdeveloped Africa
books.author: Walter Rodney
books.summary: The structural argument for African underdevelopment as a product of European capitalism — Rodney's defining work.

books.title: African Anarchism: The History of a Movement
books.author: Sam Mbah & I.E. Igariwey
books.summary: Written from Lagos — the reference text for African anarchist tradition, from acephalous societies through to contemporary movements.

books.title: The Black Jacobins
books.author: C.L.R. James
books.summary: The history of the Haitian Revolution — Toussaint L'Ouverture, San Domingo, the only successful slave revolt in modern history.

books.title: Decolonising the Mind
books.author: Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o
books.summary: Why Ngũgĩ stopped writing in English. The politics of language as the politics of mind.

books.title: Discourse on Colonialism
books.author: Aimé Césaire
books.summary: Césaire's foundational anti-colonial pamphlet — the boomerang of colonial violence returning to Europe as fascism.

books.title: Black Marxism: The Making of the Black Radical Tradition
books.author: Cedric J. Robinson
books.summary: Robinson's case for racial capitalism, and for a Black radical tradition older than Marxism.

books.title: Sister Outsider
books.author: Audre Lorde
books.summary: Fifteen essays and speeches from Lorde — Black, lesbian, mother, warrior, poet — on intersectional refusal.

books.title: Are Prisons Obsolete?
books.author: Angela Y. Davis
books.summary: Davis's introductory case for prison abolition — short, urgent, foundational.

books.title: Necropolitics
books.author: Achille Mbembe
books.summary: The book-length expansion of the 2003 essay — sovereignty as the power to dictate who lives and who dies.

books.title: Pan-African Social Ecology: Speeches, Conversations, and Essays
books.author: Modibo Kadalie
books.publisher: Daraja Press / On Our Own Authority!
books.summary: Kadalie on autonomous mass democracy and African ecological traditions — interviews with Andrew Zonneveld.

books.title: Anarchism and the Black Revolution
books.author: Lorenzo Kom'boa Ervin
books.summary: The 2021 expanded edition of Ervin's foundational pamphlet — with new afterword and analysis of contemporary movements.

external_shops.name: Bookmarks (London)
external_shops.what: UK's only socialist bookshop — strong Black radical section, zines, posters.

external_shops.name: AK Press
external_shops.what: Worker-run publisher and distributor — anarchist + Black liberation books, audio, merch.

external_shops.name: PM Press
external_shops.what: Books, zines, music, films — anarchism, Black studies, prison abolition.

external_shops.name: Casa de las Américas
external_shops.what: Cuban publisher with a deep Pan-African / decolonial catalogue.

external_shops.name: Africa Is a Country
external_shops.what: Editorial collective publishing diaspora-led essays, podcasts, criticism.

external_shops.name: Chimurenga
external_shops.what: Pan-African chronicle, library, and pop-up press — books, music, archives.

external_shops.name: Verso Books
external_shops.what: Independent radical publisher — anti-colonial, marxist, abolitionist titles.

external_shops.name: Cassava Republic Press
external_shops.what: Africa-rooted independent publisher — fiction, non-fiction, poetry from across the continent.

external_shops.name: Daraja Press
external_shops.what: Decolonial publisher — strong list on African political thought, ecology, autonomy.

external_shops.name: Soundway Records
external_shops.what: African + diasporic music label and reissue house — afrobeat, highlife, dub, jazz.

external_shops.name: Strut Records
external_shops.what: Long-running reissue label — Sun Ra, Mulatu Astatke, Tony Allen, William Onyeabor.

external_shops.name: Awesome Tapes from Africa
external_shops.what: Brian Shimkovitz's blog/label reissuing African cassettes — Hailu Mergia onwards.

grants.title: Cultural & Artistic Responses to Environmental Change
Resilience grants for women-, girl-, trans- and intersex-led groups
